stories.headline: Why Rising Cybersecurity Budgets Must Deliver Measurable Risk Reduction
stories.summary: This analysis challenges organizations to evaluate their security spending beyond operational speed, emphasizing the need for measurable risk reduction. It highlights the critical gap between rising budgets and actual security outcomes in modern threat landscapes.
stories.original_title: Why you may want to question how your security budget is being spent — Why rising cybersecurity budgets must deliver measurable risk reduction, not just faster operations.
stories.context: Imagine you spend a lot of money on a home security system, but you never check if it actually stops burglars; you just assume it works because you paid for it. For decades, companies have done the same with computer security, buying expensive software and hiring experts without ever measuring if they are truly safer from hackers. This story highlights a growing frustration where businesses are spending billions more on digital protection, yet cyberattacks are still happening at the same rate or worse. The core issue is that spending money does not automatically equal safety, so leaders are now being forced to prove that their security budget is actually reducing the chance of a data breach, rather than just keeping the IT team busy.
stories.why_it_matters: Companies are spending more money on digital security, but it is not always stopping hackers. This story pushes businesses to prove that their spending actually makes them safer, not just faster at fixing problems. If they don't fix this gap, they risk losing customer trust and suffering major data breaches.
stories.other_side: Some experts argue that measuring security risk is nearly impossible because threats change too quickly. They say focusing on speed and response time is more practical than trying to calculate exact risk numbers.

stories.headline: Turning Old Tablets Into Premium Self-Hosted Photo Displays
stories.summary: A practical guide demonstrates how to repurpose obsolete tablets into ad-free, subscription-free digital photo frames using Immich Kiosk. This solution offers a superior, cost-effective alternative to commercial smart displays by leveraging existing hardware.
stories.original_title: I turned my old tablet into a self-hosted photo display, and it’s better than anything you can buy — I turned an old tablet into a premium photo frame with no ads, subscriptions or junk with Immich Kiosk.
stories.context: Most people eventually upgrade their smartphones or tablets, leaving behind older devices that still work perfectly fine but are too slow for modern apps. Instead of throwing these working gadgets into landfills where they create toxic waste, tech enthusiasts discovered they can be turned into simple digital picture frames. This guide shows how to install free software called Immich Kiosk on these old tablets to automatically display photos from your phone without any ads or monthly fees. It is a clever way to reduce electronic waste and save money by giving old technology a second life as a beautiful, ad-free display for your home.
stories.why_it_matters: This gives people a cheap way to make old electronics useful again instead of throwing them away. It helps reduce electronic waste by turning broken or unused tablets into nice photo frames. It also saves money because users do not have to pay for expensive smart displays or monthly subscriptions.
stories.other_side: Critics might say that setting up this software is too technical for average users who just want a plug-and-play device. There is also the concern that using old hardware could lead to security issues if the software is not updated properly.

stories.headline: Havoc Secures $100M for Autonomous Drone Boat Technology
stories.summary: Autonomous marine technology firm Havoc has closed a $100 million funding round to scale its drone boat solutions amid geopolitical tensions in the Strait of Hormuz. The company faces stiff competition from established rivals like Anduril and Saronic in the defense tech sector.
stories.original_title: Meet The Former Burmese Refugee Vying To Be The U.S. Military’s Go-To Drone Guy — Interest in drone boats is surging as the crisis in the Strait of Hormuz drags on. Havoc, which just closed a $100 million funding round, claims it has the perfect tech to help but faces stiff headwinds from bigger rivals like Anduril and Saronic.
stories.context: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow strip of ocean between Iran and Oman that acts as a global chokepoint, meaning most of the world’s oil passes through this tiny space. Because of rising tensions between countries in the Middle East, there is a fear that ships carrying oil could be attacked or blocked, which would cause gas prices to skyrocket worldwide. To protect these vital shipping lanes without risking human sailors' lives, defense companies like Havoc are building autonomous boats that can patrol the water on their own. Havoc recently raised $100 million to build more of these robot boats because governments are desperate for safer, cheaper ways to monitor dangerous waters during times of conflict.
stories.why_it_matters: The world is seeing more conflict in key shipping areas like the Strait of Hormuz. This company is raising huge funds to build robot boats that can patrol waters without humans on board. This technology could change how nations protect their borders and keep trade routes open during crises.
stories.other_side: Opponents worry that autonomous weapons could escalate conflicts accidentally if the machines malfunction. There are also ethical concerns about removing human judgment from military decisions entirely.

stories.headline: Broadridge Launches Infrastructure for Tokenized Securities
stories.summary: Broadridge has unveiled integrated infrastructure designed to help institutional firms scale both digital and traditional assets on a single platform. Building on its Digital Ledger Repo platform, this move addresses the growing demand for tokenized securities in financial markets.
stories.original_title: Broadridge Announces Integrated Infrastructure for Tokenized Securities — Building on its market-leading Digital Ledger Repo platform, Broadridge delivers the infrastructure that institutional firms need to scale digital and traditional assets on a single platform NEW YORK, May 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Broadridge Financial Solution…
stories.context: Traditionally, when you buy stocks or bonds, you need a middleman company to keep track of who owns what, which makes the process slow and expensive. Recently, people have started using "tokenized securities," which means turning traditional financial assets into digital tokens on a computer network, similar to how Bitcoin works but for real-world investments. Broadridge, a company that helps banks manage money, has built a new system that allows these digital tokens and regular paper-based investments to exist on the same platform. This is important because it lets big investors mix old-school investments with new digital ones easily, making the financial market faster and more efficient for everyone involved.
stories.why_it_matters: Money markets are starting to use "tokens," which are digital versions of traditional assets like stocks or bonds. This new system helps big banks handle these digital assets safely alongside regular money. It makes investing in these new types of assets easier and more secure for everyone involved.
stories.other_side: Some financial experts fear that tokenized assets could be risky if the underlying technology fails or is hacked. Others worry that this trend might favor large tech-savvy banks over smaller, traditional financial institutions.

stories.headline: Trimble Enables Centimeter-Accurate 3D Modeling of Disappearing Glaciers
stories.summary: Trimble is providing advanced GNSS positioning technology to create precise 3D models of tropical ice fields, specifically Puncak Jaya glaciers. This data serves as a critical baseline for tracking environmental changes using high-precision geospatial tech.
stories.original_title: Trimble Technology Enables First Centimeter-Accurate 3D Model of Disappearing Puncak Jaya Glaciers — Data provides baseline measurement for tracking change at one of Earth's last tropical ice fields WESTMINSTER, Colo., May 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Trimble today announced its continued support of Project Pressure by providing advanced GNSS positioning technol…
stories.context: Glaciers are massive rivers of ice that move very slowly, and scientists use them as a thermometer for the planet because they melt quickly when the Earth gets warmer. Puncak Jaya is a unique glacier located near the equator in Indonesia, making it one of the few tropical ice fields left on Earth, and it is disappearing rapidly due to climate change. Trimble, a company that makes high-precision GPS tools for construction and farming, is using its technology to take incredibly detailed 3D maps of these glaciers down to the centimeter. By creating this exact baseline map now, scientists can compare future photos to see exactly how much ice is lost over time, providing hard proof of environmental changes.
stories.why_it_matters: Glaciers are melting fast due to climate change, and we need exact data to understand how bad it is getting. This project uses high-tech GPS tools to create perfect 3D maps of disappearing ice. This information helps scientists predict sea-level rise and plan for future environmental disasters.
stories.other_side: Some argue that building precise models of remote glaciers is too expensive compared to using satellite data. There is also debate over whether this specific data will lead to actual policy changes or just more academic research.

stories.headline: Agentic AI Emerges as the Next Major Cybersecurity Blind Spot
stories.summary: As agentic AI systems begin executing tasks and consuming data in production environments without direct human oversight, they present new security vulnerabilities. The industry must address these blind spots as AI agents operate autonomously across organizational networks.
stories.original_title: Why Agentic AI Is Security's Next Blind Spot — Agentic AI is already running in production environments across many organizations today. It is executing tasks, consuming data, and taking actions — most likely without meaningful involvement from the security team. The industry conversation has largely fram…
stories.context: Artificial Intelligence (AI) used to be like a calculator: you typed in a question, and it gave you an answer while you watched. Now, we are moving toward "agentic AI," which are programs that can make decisions and perform tasks on their own without you holding their hand every step of the way. While this sounds helpful, it creates a scary security problem because hackers could trick these autonomous robots into stealing data or breaking into systems before humans even notice something is wrong. Companies are currently scrambling to figure out how to lock down these smart agents, because once an AI starts acting independently, it becomes much harder to control or stop if it goes rogue.
stories.why_it_matters: AI programs are now doing tasks on their own without people watching every step. This creates new security holes because hackers could trick these AI agents into stealing data or breaking systems. Companies need to find ways to watch these AI workers to prevent serious cyberattacks.
stories.other_side: Some developers argue that adding strict human oversight slows down the benefits of AI too much. They believe that trusting the AI to follow its rules is efficient enough and that constant monitoring is unnecessary.

stories.headline: AI Startup Uses Artificial Intelligence to Fix Unfilled Prescription Crisis
stories.summary: Forus, a startup valued at $1 billion, is leveraging AI to streamline the administrative processing of prescriptions, ensuring patients receive necessary medications. The technology targets the unglamorous but critical backend work that often delays patient care.
stories.original_title: Billions In Prescriptions Go Unfilled. This Startup Is Using AI To Fix That. — Forus reached $1 billion valuation by dealing with the unglamorous admin work of processing scrips so patients can get the drugs they need.
stories.context: Getting a prescription filled at a pharmacy often involves hours of boring paperwork, insurance checks, and phone calls between doctors and pharmacists, which causes delays for patients who need medicine urgently. A startup called Forus uses artificial intelligence to automate this dull, back-office work by reading prescriptions and handling the administrative hurdles automatically. This technology acts like a super-fast assistant that clears the path so human pharmacists can focus on helping patients instead of fighting with computers. By fixing this hidden bottleneck, Forus ensures that people get their life-saving medications faster, proving that AI isn't just for fancy robots but also for solving everyday bureaucratic problems.
stories.why_it_matters: Many patients wait too long to get their medicine because of boring paperwork delays. A new AI startup is automating this behind-the-scenes work to get drugs to people faster. This saves lives by ensuring critical treatments are not stuck in administrative bottlenecks.
stories.other_side: Doctors worry that relying on AI for medical paperwork might lead to errors in patient records. There is also concern that this technology could replace jobs for administrative staff who process prescriptions daily.

stories.headline: The Hidden AI Strategies Already Operating Within Your Company
stories.summary: Experts reveal that many organizations have unofficial AI strategies in place, often driven by individual departments rather than centralized IT. This piece explores the human-AI interface and how decentralized adoption is reshaping corporate innovation without formal mandates.
stories.original_title: Your company already has an AI strategy. You just didn’t choose it — Dr. Tomas Chamorro-Premuzic is an international authority in people analytics, talent management, leadership development, and the Human-AI interface. He is the Chief Innovation Officer at Manpower Group, co-founder of Deeper Signals and Metaprofiling, and Pro…
stories.context: Usually, when a large company wants to use new technology like Artificial Intelligence, the top bosses write a strict plan and tell all the employees how to use it. However, many workers are secretly installing AI tools on their own computers to help them finish their daily tasks faster, creating a "shadow" strategy that the main IT department doesn't officially know about. This happens because employees see how useful AI is and want to use it immediately, even if the company hasn't approved it yet. This trend shows that innovation is often driven by individual workers trying to be more efficient, forcing companies to eventually catch up and create official rules for these unofficial practices.
stories.why_it_matters: Employees are secretly using AI tools to do their jobs better, even if bosses don't officially allow it. This shows that AI is changing how work gets done from the bottom up, not just top down. Companies need to adapt to this reality instead of trying to stop it.
stories.other_side: Managers worry that unofficial AI use creates security risks and inconsistent results across the team. They argue that without a central plan, some employees might misuse the technology or leak private company data.

stories.headline: Nasdaq Partners with Infrastructure Capital Advisors to Launch New ETF
stories.summary: Infrastructure Capital Advisors has partnered with Nasdaq to launch its first ETF, targeting high monthly income with Nasdaq equity upside exposure. This collaboration leverages Nasdaq's market infrastructure to offer actively managed strategies focused on infrastructure sectors.
stories.original_title: Infrastructure Capital Advisors Partners with Nasdaq to Launch its First Nasdaq ETF: Infrastructure Capital Nasdaq Option Income ETF (QVOL) — Actively managed strategy targets high monthly income with Nasdaq equity upside exposure NEW YORK, May 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC (Infrastructure Capital), a leading provider of investment management solutions designed to me…
stories.context: An Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) is like a basket that holds many different investments together, allowing regular people to buy a small piece of many companies at once. Infrastructure Capital Advisors wanted to create a new type of basket that focuses on physical assets like bridges, roads, and power plants, which tend to pay out steady cash to investors. They partnered with Nasdaq, the famous stock exchange, to launch this fund so that it could be bought and sold easily by anyone with a brokerage account. This collaboration brings together Nasdaq's trading platform with specialized investment ideas, offering investors a way to earn regular income while betting on the growth of essential physical infrastructure.
stories.why_it_matters: Investors are looking for steady income from infrastructure projects like bridges and power grids. This new fund combines stock market growth with regular cash payments to investors. It offers a new way for everyday people to invest in essential physical assets.
stories.other_side: Financial critics warn that infrastructure investments can be slow to grow and may not beat other stock market options. There is also the risk that these projects face delays or cost overruns, which could hurt investor returns.

stories.headline: CGI Announces Leadership Transition Amid Tech Sector Shifts
stories.summary: Global IT services firm CGI has appointed Tim Hurlebaus as its new President and CEO, marking a significant leadership transition. This change occurs as the company navigates evolving demands in digital transformation and enterprise technology services.
stories.original_title: CGI announces Leadership Transition — Stock Market Symbols GIB.A (TSX) GIB (NYSE) cgi.com/newsroom Tim Hurlebaus appointed President and Chief Executive Officer MONTRÉAL, May 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ - The CGI (NYSE: GIB) (TSX: GIB.A) Board of Directors announces the appointment of Tim Hurlebaus as …
stories.context: CGI is a massive global company that helps other businesses fix their computers, manage their data, and move into the digital age. Like many large corporations, it needs strong leadership to navigate the fast-changing world of technology, especially as clients demand more advanced digital services than ever before. Tim Hurlebaus has been appointed as the new President and CEO to take charge during this critical transition period. His job is to steer the ship through these industry shifts, ensuring the company stays competitive and continues to provide reliable tech support to its huge list of international clients.
stories.why_it_matters: Big tech companies are changing leaders to stay ahead in a fast-moving industry. CGI is getting a new boss to help guide the firm through changes in digital services. This shift shows how companies must adapt their leadership to meet new customer demands.
stories.other_side: Some employees and clients might worry that a new leader will change company culture or drop important projects. There is always uncertainty about whether a new CEO can successfully navigate complex global markets.
